skills.skill: Data Structures (Arrays, Linked Lists, Trees, Graphs)
skills.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply data structures including arrays, lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced data structures for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.5). Students should implement and analyze arrays, linked lists (singly/doubly), stacks, queues, binary trees, binary search trees, heaps, hash tables, and graphs. Key concepts: time/space complexity of operations (insert, delete, search, access), choosing appropriate structures for different problems, tree traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder, BFS, DFS), and graph representations (adjacency list, adjacency matrix). Vocabulary: data structure, array, linked list, tree, graph, node, edge, hash table, heap, BST, traversal, Big-O. Common errors: pointer/reference manipulation errors, not handling edge cases (empty structures), and choosing inefficient data structures.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply algorithm design strategies including divide and conquer, recursion, and efficiency
skills.prompt: This topic covers algorithm design strategies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.4). Students should apply divide and conquer, greedy algorithms, dynamic programming, and recursive strategies. Key concepts: Big-O notation (O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(2^n)), analyzing time and space complexity, recurrence relations (Master Theorem), and comparing algorithmic approaches. Key algorithms: merge sort, quick sort, Dijkstra's, binary search. Vocabulary: algorithm, complexity, Big-O, divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming, recursion, memoization, optimization, brute force. Common errors: incorrect complexity analysis, not recognizing overlapping subproblems for DP, and stack overflow from deep recursion.

skills.teks_description: Identify and use concepts of object-oriented design including encapsulation and abstraction
skills.prompt: This topic covers OOP principles for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.6D). Students should master the four pillars: encapsulation (bundling data and methods, access control), abstraction (hiding implementation details), inheritance (creating class hierarchies), and polymorphism (method overriding, interfaces). Key concepts: classes and objects, constructors, getters/setters, superclass/subclass, method overriding, abstract classes, interfaces, composition vs. inheritance, and SOLID principles. Vocabulary: encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, class, object, interface, abstract, override, composition. Common errors: overusing inheritance (prefer composition), breaking encapsulation, and tight coupling between classes.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply software design principles and common design patterns
skills.prompt: This topic covers software design patterns for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.6). Students should recognize and apply common patterns. Creational: Singleton (one instance), Factory (object creation), Builder (complex construction). Structural: Adapter (interface compatibility), Decorator (dynamic behavior), Composite (tree structures). Behavioral: Observer (event notification), Strategy (interchangeable algorithms), Iterator (sequential access). Key concepts: when to apply each pattern, pattern intent and structure, and avoiding pattern overuse. Vocabulary: design pattern, Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy, Decorator, MVC, anti-pattern, refactoring. Common errors: forcing patterns where they don't fit, overengineering, and confusing similar patterns.

skills.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Apply software development methodologies and engineering principles
skills.prompt: This topic covers software development methodologies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.5). Students should understand Agile/Scrum, Waterfall, and iterative development methodologies. Key concepts: requirements gathering, system design, code reviews, documentation, technical debt, DRY/KISS/YAGNI principles, clean code practices, and software development lifecycle (SDLC). Vocabulary: Agile, Scrum, sprint, user story, requirement, specification, refactoring, technical debt, code review, CI/CD, deployment. Students should follow best practices for naming, commenting, and organizing code. Common errors: skipping requirements analysis, inadequate documentation, and accumulating technical debt.

skills.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Create and manipulate data using database management systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers database concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.7). Students should design relational databases and write SQL queries. Key SQL: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, JOIN (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT), GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, WHERE, and aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX). Key concepts: relational model, tables/rows/columns, primary and foreign keys, normalization (1NF, 2NF, 3NF), ER diagrams, and CRUD operations. Vocabulary: database, table, row, column, primary key, foreign key, join, query, normalization, SQL, CRUD, schema, index. Common errors: SQL injection vulnerabilities, denormalized designs, missing foreign key constraints, and Cartesian products from missing JOIN conditions.

skills.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Develop web-based applications using markup and scripting languages
skills.prompt: This topic covers web development technologies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.8). Students should create web pages using HTML (structure), CSS (styling), and JavaScript (interactivity). Key HTML: semantic tags, forms, tables, links, images. Key CSS: selectors, box model, flexbox, grid, responsive design. Key JS: DOM manipulation, event handlers, fetch API, and basic frameworks. Key concepts: separation of concerns, responsive design, accessibility, and client-server interaction. Vocabulary: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, DOM, selector, responsive, flexbox, grid, event, API, AJAX, framework. Common errors: inline styling (prefer CSS classes), not making sites responsive, accessibility issues, and XSS vulnerabilities.

skills.teks_description: Demonstrate proper digital etiquette, responsible use of software and version management tools
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced Git usage for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5C). Students should manage code collaboratively using Git and GitHub/GitLab. Key concepts: branching strategies (feature branches, Git Flow), merge vs. rebase, pull requests/code reviews, resolving conflicts, .gitignore, tags and releases, and collaboration workflows. Advanced: cherry-pick, stash, bisect, interactive staging, and hooks. Vocabulary: repository, branch, merge, rebase, pull request, conflict, stash, tag, release, fork, clone, remote, upstream. Common errors: committing to wrong branch, force-pushing to shared branches, large binary files in repo, and leaked credentials.

skills.teks_description: Test program solutions with valid and invalid test data; §126.33.c.4(J) debug using error messages
skills.prompt: This topic covers software testing for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.4I, §126.33.c.4J). Students should write and run unit tests, integration tests, and use debugging tools. Key concepts: test-driven development (TDD), unit testing frameworks (unittest, pytest), test coverage, boundary value analysis, equivalence partitioning, mocking, and systematic debugging with debuggers and logging. Vocabulary: unit test, integration test, TDD, assertion, mock, stub, coverage, regression test, edge case, boundary value. Students should test with valid, invalid, and edge-case data. Common errors: testing only happy paths, brittle tests, insufficient coverage, and testing implementation instead of behavior.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply software architecture and modular design principles
skills.prompt: This topic covers software architecture principles for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.6). Students should understand and apply architectural patterns: MVC (Model-View-Controller), microservices, layered architecture, event-driven architecture, and client-server. Key concepts: separation of concerns, modularity, coupling and cohesion, scalability, maintainability, and choosing architecture based on requirements. Vocabulary: architecture, MVC, microservices, monolith, API, layer, module, coupling, cohesion, scalability, middleware. Common errors: monolithic design for complex systems, tight coupling between components, and overarchitecting simple applications.

skills.teks_description: Identify and describe the function of major hardware components and operating system concepts
skills.prompt: This topic covers OS fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.6A). Students should understand how operating systems manage hardware and software resources. Key concepts: processes and threads, memory management (stack vs. heap, virtual memory), file systems, CPU scheduling, I/O management, and the role of the kernel. Vocabulary: operating system, process, thread, kernel, memory, file system, CPU scheduling, virtual memory, stack, heap, deadlock, context switch. Common errors: confusing processes and threads, memory leaks, and race conditions in concurrent programs.

skills.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Understand networking concepts and protocols
skills.prompt: This topic covers networking concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.9). Students should understand how computers communicate over networks. Key concepts: OSI and TCP/IP models, IP addressing (IPv4/IPv6), DNS, HTTP/HTTPS protocols, TCP vs. UDP, ports, sockets, RESTful APIs, and basic network security. Vocabulary: network, protocol, IP address, DNS, TCP, UDP, HTTP, HTTPS, port, socket, API, REST, packet, bandwidth, latency, firewall. Common errors: confusing TCP and UDP use cases, hardcoding IP addresses, ignoring HTTPS for security, and not handling network timeouts.

skills.teks_description: Investigate privacy and security measures in computing
skills.prompt: This topic covers cybersecurity fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5D). Students should understand common security threats and protective measures. Key concepts: authentication vs. authorization, encryption (symmetric/asymmetric), hashing (SHA, bcrypt for passwords), common vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF), secure coding practices, HTTPS/TLS, and the principle of least privilege. Vocabulary: authentication, authorization, encryption, hashing, vulnerability, exploit, firewall, SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, TLS, certificate, two-factor authentication. Common errors: storing passwords in plaintext, SQL injection from unsanitized input, and security through obscurity.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science III: Apply advanced programming concepts including concurrency
skills.prompt: This topic covers concurrent programming for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.35.c.5). Students should understand threads, processes, and asynchronous programming. Key concepts: multithreading vs. multiprocessing, race conditions, deadlocks, mutexes/locks, semaphores, thread safety, Python's GIL, async/await pattern, and parallel algorithms. Vocabulary: concurrency, parallelism, thread, process, race condition, deadlock, mutex, semaphore, synchronization, async, await, GIL, thread pool. Common errors: race conditions from shared state, deadlocks from lock ordering, and not understanding Python's GIL limitation for CPU-bound tasks.

skills.teks_description: Mobile Application Development TEKS: Design and develop mobile applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers mobile application development for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.39). Students should understand mobile app architecture, UI/UX considerations for mobile, and development approaches. Key concepts: native vs. cross-platform development, responsive design for various screen sizes, touch interaction patterns, app lifecycle, local storage, API consumption, and app store deployment. Vocabulary: mobile app, native, cross-platform, responsive, UI, UX, lifecycle, API, SDK, emulator, deploy, React Native, Flutter, Swift, Kotlin. Common errors: not considering different screen sizes, poor touch target sizing, excessive battery/data usage, and ignoring platform design guidelines.

skills.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Understand cloud computing concepts and services
skills.prompt: This topic covers cloud computing concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.10). Students should understand cloud service models and deployment patterns. Key concepts: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS models, major providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), virtual machines vs. containers (Docker), serverless computing, cloud storage, databases as a service, CI/CD pipelines, and cost management. Vocabulary: cloud, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, VM, container, Docker, serverless, AWS, Azure, GCP, scaling, load balancer, CDN, DevOps. Common errors: leaving resources running (cost overruns), poor security configuration, vendor lock-in, and not understanding shared responsibility model.

skills.teks_description: Investigate computing and computing-related advancements including AI
skills.prompt: This topic covers AI fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5E). Students should understand what AI is, its capabilities and limitations, and basic approaches. Key concepts: rule-based systems vs. machine learning, supervised/unsupervised/reinforcement learning, neural networks (high level), natural language processing, computer vision, AI ethics and bias, and practical AI tools and APIs. Vocabulary: artificial intelligence, machine learning, neural network, training data, model, prediction, classification, NLP, computer vision, bias, ethics, AGI, narrow AI. Common errors: overstating AI capabilities, ignoring bias in training data, and confusing AI with automation.

skills.teks_description: Investigate computing advancements including machine learning and data science
skills.prompt: This topic covers machine learning fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5E). Students should understand how ML models learn from data. Key concepts: supervised learning (regression, classification), unsupervised learning (clustering, dimensionality reduction), training/validation/test splits, overfitting vs. underfitting, feature engineering, and evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1). Key algorithms: linear regression, decision trees, k-nearest neighbors, k-means clustering. Vocabulary: machine learning, model, training, testing, feature, label, overfitting, underfitting, cross-validation, accuracy, precision, recall. Common errors: training on test data, not normalizing features, ignoring class imbalance, and overfitting to training data.

skills.teks_description: Create interactive input interfaces with relevant user prompts and good design
skills.prompt: This topic covers user interface and experience design for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.3B). Students should create intuitive, accessible, and visually appealing interfaces. Key concepts: usability heuristics (Nielsen's 10), visual hierarchy, consistency, feedback, accessibility (WCAG guidelines), color theory, typography, whitespace, responsive design, and user testing. Vocabulary: UI, UX, usability, accessibility, wireframe, prototype, heuristic, responsive, WCAG, contrast ratio, affordance, user flow, persona. Common errors: inconsistent interfaces, poor color contrast (accessibility), information overload, and designing for developers instead of users.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply programming interfaces and API integration patterns
skills.prompt: This topic covers API design and integration for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.4). Students should create and consume RESTful APIs, understand HTTP methods, and handle data formats. Key concepts: REST principles, HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH), status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500), JSON data format, API authentication (API keys, OAuth, JWT), rate limiting, and API documentation. Vocabulary: API, REST, endpoint, HTTP method, status code, JSON, authentication, authorization, token, rate limit, CRUD, webhook. Common errors: not handling error responses, ignoring rate limits, insecure API key storage, and inconsistent endpoint naming.
